context.pr_body:
<!-- To ensure we can review your pull request promptly please complete this template entirely. -->

<!-- Please reference the issue number here. You can replace "Fixes" with "Closes" if it makes more sense. -->
Fixes #162

Changes proposed in this pull request:
<!-- Please list all changes/additions here. -->
- Add `jsonl_to_markdown.py` utility script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format
- Script extracts the 'text' field from each line in a JSONL file and saves it as a separate Markdown file
- Include proper error handling for JSON decoding and file operations
- Add example usage and documentation

## Before submitting

<!-- Please complete this checklist BEFORE submitting your PR to speed along the review process. -->
- [x] I've read and followed all steps in the [Making a pull request](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md#making-a-pull-request) section of the `CONTRIBUTING` docs.
- [x] I've updated or added relevant docstrings following the syntax described in the [Writing docstrings](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md#writing-docstrings) section of the `CONTRIBUTING` docs.
- [x] If this PR fixes a bug, I've added a test that will fail without my fix.
- [x] If this PR adds a new feature, I've added tests that sufficiently cover my new functionality.

fixes  #162

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README

A toolkit for converting PDFs and other image-based document formats into clean, readable, plain text format. Try the online demo: [https://olmocr.allenai.org/](https://olmocr.allenai.org/) Features: - Convert PDF, PNG, and JPEG based documents into clean Markdown - Support for equations, tables, handwriting, and complex formatting - Automatically removes headers and footers - Convert into text with a natural reading order, even in the presence of figures, multi-column layouts, and insets - Efficient, less than $200 USD per million pages converted - (Based on a 7B parameter VLM, so it requires a GPU) ### News - May 19, 2025 - v0.1.68 - [olmOCR-Bench](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/tree/main/olmocr/bench) launch, scoring 77.4. Launch includes 2 point performance boost in olmOCR pipeline due to bug fixes with prompts. - Mar 17, 2025 - v0.1.60 - Performance improvements due to better temperature selection in sampling. - Feb 25, 2025 - v0.1.58 - Initial public launch and demo. ### Benchmark [**olmOCR-Bench**](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/tree/main/olmocr/bench): We also ship a comprehensive benchmark suite covering over 7,000 test cases across 1,400 documents to help measure performance of OCR systems.
Model AR OSM TA OS HF MC LTT Base Overall Score
Marker v1.6.2 24.3 22.1 69.8 24.3 87.1 71.0

arguments.reason: Inspect dataset format to design converter

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
A JSONL dataset containing independent extraction records sourced from multiple PDFs. Each line is a JSON object describing a document fragment, with some lines including a 'text' field used to survey the dataset format for conversion.

### Architecture
- The file is a single JSON Lines (JSONL) collection: many independent JSON objects, one per line.
- Each object includes common metadata fields such as pdf, page, id, and type, and may include:
  - text: the extracted textual content (present in many records; this is the primary target for a converter).
  - table: a nested representation for tabular extracts (with fields like cell, left, right, top_heading, etc.).
  - math: a math expression (string) extract.
  - order, present, absent, checked, max_diffs, last_n: additional metadata describing the extraction or annotation status.
- No code or executable components exist in this file; it’s purely a data snapshot used for format inspection.

### Functions & Classes
- None — this file contains only data records (no functions, classes, or methods).

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file directly serves the goal of inspecting dataset format to design a converter. It demonstrates:
  - The JSONL structure (one JSON object per line) and consistent keys like pdf, page, id, type.
  - The emphasis on the 'text' field as a primary content carrier (e.g., lines like multi_column_miss_00 and multi_column_miss_01 contain text fields).
  - The presence of other content types (table, math, order) that may need special handling or be ignored depending on the converter’s scope.
  - The variety of metadata accompanying each record (e.g., checked, max_diffs, last_n), illustrating how richer annotations are represented in addition to plain text.
- The agent can use this as a template for building a converter that extracts lines with a text field, and optionally handles or ignores other type categories as needed.

summarization.system_prompt:
You are a code analysis assistant. Your task is to summarize source code files for an AI agent that is planning code modifications for a Pull Request.

Your summary MUST:
1. Preserve ALL exact function names, class names, and method names exactly as they appear in the source code — never rename or abbreviate them.
2. Explain the overall architecture and purpose of the file.
3. Describe the role and importance of each function, class, and method.
4. Highlight the parts most relevant to the agent's stated goal.

Format your response with these sections:
### File Overview
[1-2 sentences on the file's purpose]

### Architecture
[How the main components are organised and interact]

### Functions & Classes
[For each function/class/method: `exact_name` — description and its importance]

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
[How this file relates to the agent's reason for reading it]

arguments.reason: Check existing conversion utilities and data formats for JSONL/Markdown compatibility
arguments.expected_information: Any existing JSONL handling or markdown export logic

tool_calls.raw_result:
import argparse
import asyncio
import base64
import glob
import importlib
import os
import tempfile
from functools import partial

from pypdf import PdfReader
from tqdm import tqdm

from olmocr.data.renderpdf import render_pdf_to_base64png
from olmocr.image_utils import convert_image_to_pdf_bytes


def parse_method_arg(method_arg):
    """
    Parse a method configuration string of the form:
       method_name[:key=value[:key2=value2...]]
    Returns:
       (method_name, kwargs_dict, folder_name)
    """
    parts = method_arg.split(":")
    name = parts[0]
    kwargs = {}
    folder_name = name  # Default folder name is the method name

    for extra in parts[1:]:
        if "=" in extra:
            key, value = extra.split("=", 1)
            if key == "name":
                folder_name = value
                continue

            try:
                converted = int(value)
            except ValueError:
                try:
                    converted = float(value)
                except ValueError:
                    converted = value
            kwargs[key] = converted
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Extra argument '{extra}' is not in key=value format")

    return name, kwargs, folder_name


# Wrapper to run synchronous functions in the event loop
async def run_sync_in_executor(func, *args, **kwargs):
    """Run a synchronous function in the default executor"""
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(None, partial(func, *args, **kwargs))


async def process_pdf(pdf_path, page_num, method, kwargs, output_path, is_async):
    """Process a single PDF and save the result to output_path"""
    try:
        if is_async:
            # Run async function directly
            markdown = await method(pdf_path, page_num=page_num, **kwargs)
        else:
            # Run synchronous function in the executor
            markdown = await run_sync_in_executor(method, pdf_path, page_num=page_num, **kwargs)

        if markdown is None:
            print(f"Warning, did not get output for {os.path.basename(output_path)}")
            # Write blank to this file, so that it's marked as an error and not just skipped in evals
            with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
                out_f.write("")
            return False

        # Write the markdown to the output file
        with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
            out_f.write(markdown)

        return True
    except Exception as ex:
        print(f"Exception {str(ex)} occurred while processing {os.path.basename(output_path)}")
        # Write blank to this file, so that it's marked as an error and not just skipped in evals
        with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
            out_f.write("")
        return False


async def process_pdfs(config, pdf_directory, data_directory, repeats, remove_text, force, max_parallel=None):
    """
    Process PDFs using asyncio for both sync and async methods,
    limiting the number of concurrent tasks to max_parallel.
    """
    for candidate in config.keys():
        print(f"Starting conversion using {candidate} with kwargs: {config[candidate]['kwargs']}")
        folder_name = config[candidate]["folder_name"]
        candidate_output_dir = os.path.join(data_directory, folder_name)
        os.makedirs(candidate_output_dir, exist_ok=True)

        method = config[candidate]["method"]
        kwargs = config[candidate]["kwargs"]
        is_async = asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(method)

        # Use recursive glob to support nested PDFs
        all_pdfs = glob.glob(os.path.join(pdf_directory, "**/*.pdf"), recursive=True)
        all_pdfs.sort()

        # Prepare all tasks
        tasks = []
        task_descriptions = {}

        for pdf_path in all_pdfs:
            pdf = PdfReader(pdf_path)
            num_pages = len(pdf.pages)
            base_name = os.path.basename(pdf_path).replace(".pdf", "")
            # Determine the PDF's relative folder path (e.g. "arxiv_data") relative to pdf_directory
            relative_pdf_path = os.path.relpath(pdf_path, pdf_directory)
            pdf_relative_dir = os.path.dirname(relative_pdf_path)

            if remove_text:
                print(f"Converting {pdf_path} into images to remove text-content...")

                # Generate image files from each page
                temp_image_files = []
                try:
                    for page_num in range(1, num_pages + 1):
                        # Get base64 PNG data for the current page
                        base64_png = render_pdf_to_base64png(pdf_path, page_num, target_longest_image_dim=2048)

                        # Decode base64 and save to temporary file
                        temp_img = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("wb", suffix=".png", delete=False)
                        temp_img.write(base64.b64decode(base64_png))
                        temp_img.close()
                        temp_image_files.append(temp_img.name)

                    # Convert all images to a single PDF using our enhanced function
                    pdf_bytes = convert_image_to_pdf_bytes(temp_image_files)

                    # Write the PDF bytes to a temporary file
                    temp_pdf = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("wb", suffix=".pdf", delete=False)
                    temp_pdf.write(pdf_bytes)
                    temp_pdf.close()

                    # Update pdf_path to the new file
                    pdf_path = temp_pdf.name

                finally:
                    # Clean up temporary image files
                    for temp_file in temp_image_files:
                        try:
                            os.remove(temp_file)
                        except Exception as e:
                            print(f"Warning: Failed to remove temporary file {temp_file}: {e}")

            for repeat in range(1, repeats + 1):
                for page_num in range(1, num_pages + 1):
                    output_filename = f"{base_name}_pg{page_num}_repeat{repeat}.md"
                    # Preserve the relative folder structure in the output directory
                    candidate_pdf_dir = os.path.join(candidate_output_dir, pdf_relative_dir)
                    os.makedirs(candidate_pdf_dir, exist_ok=True)
                    output_path = os.path.join(candidate_pdf_dir, output_filename)

                    if os.path.exists(output_path) and not force:
                        print(f"Skipping {base_name}_pg{page_num}_repeat{repeat} for {candidate}, file already exists")
                        print("Rerun with --force flag to force regeneration")
                        continue

                    task = process_pdf(pdf_path, page_num, method, kwargs, output_path, is_async)
                    tasks.append(task)
                    task_descriptions[id(task)] = f"{base_name}_pg{page_num}_repeat{repeat} ({candidate})"

        # Process tasks with semaphore to limit concurrency
        semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_parallel or 1)  # Default to 1 if not specified

        async def process_with_semaphore(task):
            async with semaphore:
                return await task

        # Wrap each task with the semaphore
        limited_tasks = [process_with_semaphore(task) for task in tasks]

        # Process tasks with progress bar
        if limited_tasks:
            completed = 0
            with tqdm(total=len(limited_tasks), desc=f"Processing {candidate}") as pbar:
                for task in asyncio.as_completed(limited_tasks):
                    try:
                        result = await task
                        if result:
                            completed += 1
                    except Exception as e:
                        print(f"Task failed: {e}")
                    finally:
                        pbar.update(1)

            print(f"Completed {completed} out of {len(limited_tasks)} tasks for {candidate}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run PDF conversion using specified OCR methods and extra parameters.")
    parser.add_argument(
        "methods",
        nargs="+",
        help="Methods to run in the format method[:key=value ...]. "
        "Example: gotocr mineru:temperature=2 marker:u=3. "
        "Use 'name=folder_name' to specify a custom output folder name.",
    )
    parser.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=1, help="Number of times to repeat the conversion for each PDF.")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--dir",
        type=str,
        default=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "sample_data"),
        help="Path to the data folder in which to save outputs, pdfs should be in /pdfs folder within it.",
    )
    parser.add_argument("--force", action="store_true", default=False, help="Force regenerating of output files, even if they already exist")
    parser.add_argument("--parallel", type=int, default=1, help="Maximum number of concurrent tasks")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--remove_text",
        action="store_true",
        help="When your PDF gets processed, we will take a screenshot of it first, to erase any text content in it. This would disable document-anchoring for olmocr.",
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Mapping of method names to a tuple: (module path, function name)
    available_methods = {
        "olmocr_pipeline": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_olmocr_pipeline", "run_olmocr_pipeline"),
        "gotocr": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_gotocr", "run_gotocr"),
        "marker": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_marker", "run_marker"),
        "mineru": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_mineru", "run_mineru"),
        "chatgpt": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_chatgpt", "run_chatgpt"),
        "gemini": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_gemini", "run_gemini"),
        "mistral": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_mistral", "run_mistral"),
        "docling": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_docling", "run_docling"),
        "rolmocr": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_rolmocr", "run_rolmocr"),
        "transformers": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_transformers", "run_transformers"),
        "server": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_server", "run_server"),
    }

    # Build config by importing only requested methods.
    config = {}
    for method_arg in args.methods:
        method_name, extra_kwargs, folder_name = parse_method_arg(method_arg)
        if method_name not in available_methods:
            parser.error(f"Unknown method: {method_name}. " f"Available methods: {', '.join(available_methods.keys())}")
        module_path, function_name = available_methods[method_name]
        # Dynamically import the module and get the function.
        module = importlib.import_module(module_path)
        function = getattr(module, function_name)
        config[method_name] = {"method": function, "kwargs": extra_kwargs, "folder_name": folder_name}

    data_directory = args.dir
    pdf_directory = os.path.join(data_directory, "pdfs")

    # Run the async process function with the parallel argument
    asyncio.run(process_pdfs(config, pdf_directory, data_directory, args.repeats, args.remove_text, args.force, args.parallel))
tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
olmocr/bench/convert.py is a command-line utility that orchestrates converting PDFs into outputs produced by pluggable “methods.” It supports per-page outputs (as Markdown), optional text-removal by rendering pages to images, and concurrent processing with repeats and directory-preserving outputs.

### Architecture
- The script exposes a small, extensible workflow:
  - Parse method arguments to determine which conversion methods to run and with what parameters.
  - Dynamically import the requested methods from a predefined registry.
  - Traverse a PDFs directory (recursively), derive per-PDF metadata (pages, path), and optionally convert PDFs to images to remove text content.
  - For each PDF, page, and repeat, spawn asynchronous tasks to generate Markdown outputs, while respecting a maximum concurrency limit and showing progress via a progress bar.
- Core components:
  - Argument parsing and method resolution (dynamic loading from available_methods).
  - Async task management with a semaphore to cap parallelism.
  - Individual PDF processing logic (process_pdf) that handles synchronous vs asynchronous methods and robust error handling.
  - Optional “remove_text” flow that converts PDF pages to base64 PNGs, reassembles into a new PDF, and uses that for downstream processing.
- Outputs are Markdown files (.md) that preserve the relative input folder structure under the target data directory.

### Functions & Classes
- parse_method_arg — Parse a method configuration string of the form: method_name[:key=value[:key2=value2...]] Returns: (method_name, kwargs_dict, folder_name)
  - Purpose: Break down a user-provided method argument into the method name, a dictionary of keyword arguments, and a folder name for output organization.
  - Importance: Drives dynamic method loading and output organization; supports type casting for numeric values and a special name key to customize output folders.

- run_sync_in_executor — Wrapper to run synchronous functions in the event loop
  - Purpose: Allow running blocking (synchronous) functions in the asyncio event loop’s default executor, so both sync and async methods can be mixed.
  - Importance: Enables uniform handling of methods that aren’t async, ensuring the overall pipeline can be fully asynchronous while still supporting legacy/sync converters.

- process_pdf — Process a single PDF page and save the result to output_path
  - Purpose: Invoke the selected conversion method (synchronously or asynchronously depending on the method), obtain Markdown content, and write it to disk. Handles None results and exceptions by writing a blank output to mark errors.
  - Importance: The core unit of work that produces the per-page Markdown outputs; includes robust error handling and output management to support downstream evaluation tooling.

- process_pdfs — Orchestrate processing of all PDFs across methods, with repeats and concurrency control
  - Purpose: For each configured method, iterate all PDFs (recursively), optionally render pages to images to remove text content, then kick off per-page-per-repeat tasks. Manages output directory structure, existence checks (with force), task creation, and progress reporting.
  - Importance: Central workflow controller that ties together discovery, per-PDF processing, text-removal flow, concurrency limits, and user feedback. It’s the module’s main orchestration point.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file contains the Markdown export logic used to generate per-page outputs (output filenames end with .md), which is directly relevant to JSONL/Markdown compatibility concerns the agent is checking.
- It demonstrates how existing conversion utilities are wired together:
  - Dynamic method loading (via available_methods and parse_method_arg) shows how JSONL/Markdown workflows could be extended or validated for compatibility with external tooling.
  - The remove_text flow uses render_pdf_to_base64png and convert_image_to_pdf_bytes to produce a text-free PDF, a path that may impact downstream JSONL formatting expectations if image-based or text-extracted data is used.
  - The concurrent processing and output organization logic informs how to structure or modify export formats and how to handle multi-page, multi-repeat outputs in a stable, observable way.
- Key touchpoints for modification:
  - process_pdf: where Markdown content is produced and written; a primary target for ensuring JSONL/Markdown compatibility or adding alternative export formats.
  - process_pdfs: where per-page/per-repeat expansion and output path structure are decided; useful for adjusting how data is serialized or how metadata is attached to outputs.
  - remove_text workflow: affects the content that downstream tooling would parse or index; relevant if JSONL embeddings or Markdown metadata need to reflect text-free vs. text-rich pages.
- Overall, this file is a practical centerpiece for understanding how PDFs are converted and exported to Markdown, with explicit hooks for extending formats or adjusting export semantics to fit a JSONL/Markdown compatibility plan.

import asyncio
import base64
import glob
import importlib
import os
import tempfile
from functools import partial

from pypdf import PdfReader
from tqdm import tqdm

from olmocr.data.renderpdf import render_pdf_to_base64png
from olmocr.image_utils import convert_image_to_pdf_bytes


def parse_method_arg(method_arg):
    """
    Parse a method configuration string of the form:
       method_name[:key=value[:key2=value2...]]
    Returns:
       (method_name, kwargs_dict, folder_name)
    """
    parts = method_arg.split(":")
    name = parts[0]
    kwargs = {}
    folder_name = name  # Default folder name is the method name

    for extra in parts[1:]:
        if "=" in extra:
            key, value = extra.split("=", 1)
            if key == "name":
                folder_name = value
                continue

            try:
                converted = int(value)
            except ValueError:
                try:
                    converted = float(value)
                except ValueError:
                    converted = value
            kwargs[key] = converted
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Extra argument '{extra}' is not in key=value format")

    return name, kwargs, folder_name


# Wrapper to run synchronous functions in the event loop
async def run_sync_in_executor(func, *args, **kwargs):
    """Run a synchronous function in the default executor"""
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(None, partial(func, *args, **kwargs))


async def process_pdf(pdf_path, page_num, method, kwargs, output_path, is_async):
    """Process a single PDF and save the result to output_path"""
    try:
        if is_async:
            # Run async function directly
            markdown = await method(pdf_path, page_num=page_num, **kwargs)
        else:
            # Run synchronous function in the executor
            markdown = await run_sync_in_executor(method, pdf_path, page_num=page_num, **kwargs)

        if markdown is None:
            print(f"Warning, did not get output for {os.path.basename(output_path)}")
            # Write blank to this file, so that it's marked as an error and not just skipped in evals
            with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
                out_f.write("")
            return False

        # Write the markdown to the output file
        with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
            out_f.write(markdown)

        return True
    except Exception as ex:
        print(f"Exception {str(ex)} occurred while processing {os.path.basename(output_path)}")
        # Write blank to this file, so that it's marked as an error and not just skipped in evals
        with open(output_path, "w") as out_f:
            out_f.write("")
        return False


async def process_pdfs(config, pdf_directory, data_directory, repeats, remove_text, force, max_parallel=None):
    """
    Process PDFs using asyncio for both sync and async methods,
    limiting the number of concurrent tasks to max_parallel.
    """
    for candidate in config.keys():
        print(f"Starting conversion using {candidate} with kwargs: {config[candidate]['kwargs']}")
        folder_name = config[candidate]["folder_name"]
        candidate_output_dir = os.path.join(data_directory, folder_name)
        os.makedirs(candidate_output_dir, exist_ok=True)

        method = config[candidate]["method"]
        kwargs = config[candidate]["kwargs"]
        is_async = asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(method)

        # Use recursive glob to support nested PDFs
        all_pdfs = glob.glob(os.path.join(pdf_directory, "**/*.pdf"), recursive=True)
        all_pdfs.sort()

        # Prepare all tasks
        tasks = []
        task_descriptions = {}

        for pdf_path in all_pdfs:
            pdf = PdfReader(pdf_path)
            num_pages = len(pdf.pages)
            base_name = os.path.basename(pdf_path).replace(".pdf", "")
            # Determine the PDF's relative folder path (e.g. "arxiv_data") relative to pdf_directory
            relative_pdf_path = os.path.relpath(pdf_path, pdf_directory)
            pdf_relative_dir = os.path.dirname(relative_pdf_path)

            if remove_text:
                print(f"Converting {pdf_path} into images to remove text-content...")

                # Generate image files from each page
                temp_image_files = []
                try:
                    for page_num in range(1, num_pages + 1):
                        # Get base64 PNG data for the current page
                        base64_png = render_pdf_to_base64png(pdf_path, page_num, target_longest_image_dim=2048)

                        # Decode base64 and save to temporary file
                        temp_img = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("wb", suffix=".png", delete=False)
                        temp_img.write(base64.b64decode(base64_png))
                        temp_img.close()
                        temp_image_files.append(temp_img.name)

                    # Convert all images to a single PDF using our enhanced function
                    pdf_bytes = convert_image_to_pdf_bytes(temp_image_files)

                    # Write the PDF bytes to a temporary file
                    temp_pdf = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("wb", suffix=".pdf", delete=False)
                    temp_pdf.write(pdf_bytes)
                    temp_pdf.close()

                    # Update pdf_path to the new file
                    pdf_path = temp_pdf.name

                finally:

                    for temp_file in temp_image_files:
                        try:

            for repeat in range(1, repeats + 1):
                for page_num in range(1, num_pages + 1):
                    output_filename = f"{base_name}_pg{page_num}_repeat{repeat}.md"
                    # Preserve the relative folder structure in the output directory
                    candidate_pdf_dir = os.path.join(candidate_output_dir, pdf_relative_dir)

                    if os.path.exists(output_path) and not force:

        # Process tasks with progress bar
        if limited_tasks:
            completed = 0
            with tqdm(total=len(limited_tasks), desc=f"Processing {candidate}") as pbar:
                for task in asyncio.as_completed(limited_tasks):
                    try:
                        result = await task
                        if result:
                            completed += 1
                    except Exception as e:
                        print(f"Task failed: {e}")
                    finally:
                        pbar.update(1)

            print(f"Completed {completed} out of {len(limited_tasks)} tasks for {candidate}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run PDF conversion using specified OCR methods and extra parameters.")
    parser.add_argument(
        "methods",
        nargs="+",
        help="Methods to run in the format method[:key=value ...]. "
        "Example: gotocr mineru:temperature=2 marker:u=3. "
        "Use 'name=folder_name' to specify a custom output folder name.",
    )
    parser.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=1, help="Number of times to repeat the conversion for each PDF.")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--dir",
        type=str,
        default=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "sample_data"),
        help="Path to the data folder in which to save outputs, pdfs should be in /pdfs folder within it.",
    )
    parser.add_argument("--force", action="store_true", default=False, help="Force regenerating of output files, even if they already exist")
    parser.add_argument("--parallel", type=int, default=1, help="Maximum number of concurrent tasks")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--remove_text",
        action="store_true",
        help="When your PDF gets processed, we will take a screenshot of it first, to erase any text content in it. This would disable document-anchoring for olmocr.",
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Mapping of method names to a tuple: (module path, function name)
    available_methods = {
        "olmocr_pipeline": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_olmocr_pipeline", "run_olmocr_pipeline"),
        "gotocr": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_gotocr", "run_gotocr"),
        "marker": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_marker", "run_marker"),
        "mineru": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_mineru", "run_mineru"),
        "chatgpt": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_chatgpt", "run_chatgpt"),
        "gemini": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_gemini", "run_gemini"),
        "mistral": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_mistral", "run_mistral"),
        "docling": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_docling", "run_docling"),
        "rolmocr": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_rolmocr", "run_rolmocr"),
        "transformers": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_transformers", "run_transformers"),
        "server": ("olmocr.bench.runners.run_server", "run_server"),
    }

    # Build config by importing only requested methods.
    config = {}
    for method_arg in args.methods:
        method_name, extra_kwargs, folder_name = parse_method_arg(method_arg)
        if method_name not in available_methods:
            parser.error(f"Unknown method: {method_name}. " f"Available methods: {', '.join(available_methods.keys())}")
        module_path, function_name = available_methods[method_name]
        # Dynamically import the module and get the function.
        module = importlib.import_module(module_path)
        function = getattr(module, function_name)

    data_directory = args.dir

    # Run the async process function with the parallel argument
    asyncio.run(process_pdfs(config, pdf_directory, data_directory, args.repeats, args.remove_text, args.force, args.parallel))

### File Overview
olmocr/bench/convert.py is a command-line utility that orchestrates converting PDFs into outputs produced by pluggable “methods.” It supports per-page outputs (as Markdown), optional text-removal by rendering pages to images, and concurrent processing with repeats and directory-preserving outputs.

### Architecture
- The script exposes a small, extensible workflow:
  - Parse method arguments to determine which conversion methods to run and with what parameters.
  - Dynamically import the requested methods from a predefined registry.
  - Traverse a PDFs directory (recursively), derive per-PDF metadata (pages, path), and optionally convert PDFs to images to remove text content.
  - For each PDF, page, and repeat, spawn asynchronous tasks to generate Markdown outputs, while respecting a maximum concurrency limit and showing progress via a progress bar.
- Core components:
  - Argument parsing and method resolution (dynamic loading from available_methods).
  - Async task management with a semaphore to cap parallelism.
  - Individual PDF processing logic (process_pdf) that handles synchronous vs asynchronous methods and robust error handling.
  - Optional “remove_text” flow that converts PDF pages to base64 PNGs, reassembles into a new PDF, and uses that for downstream processing.
- Outputs are Markdown files (.md) that preserve the relative input folder structure under the target data directory.

### Functions & Classes
- parse_method_arg — Parse a method configuration string of the form: method_name[:key=value[:key2=value2...]] Returns: (method_name, kwargs_dict, folder_name)
  - Purpose: Break down a user-provided method argument into the method name, a dictionary of keyword arguments, and a folder name for output organization.
  - Importance: Drives dynamic method loading and output organization; supports type casting for numeric values and a special name key to customize output folders.

- run_sync_in_executor — Wrapper to run synchronous functions in the event loop
  - Purpose: Allow running blocking (synchronous) functions in the asyncio event loop’s default executor, so both sync and async methods can be mixed.
  - Importance: Enables uniform handling of methods that aren’t async, ensuring the overall pipeline can be fully asynchronous while still supporting legacy/sync converters.

- process_pdf — Process a single PDF page and save the result to output_path
  - Purpose: Invoke the selected conversion method (synchronously or asynchronously depending on the method), obtain Markdown content, and write it to disk. Handles None results and exceptions by writing a blank output to mark errors.
  - Importance: The core unit of work that produces the per-page Markdown outputs; includes robust error handling and output management to support downstream evaluation tooling.

- process_pdfs — Orchestrate processing of all PDFs across methods, with repeats and concurrency control
  - Purpose: For each configured method, iterate all PDFs (recursively), optionally render pages to images to remove text content, then kick off per-page-per-repeat tasks. Manages output directory structure, existence checks (with force), task creation, and progress reporting.
  - Importance: Central workflow controller that ties together discovery, per-PDF processing, text-removal flow, concurrency limits, and user feedback. It’s the module’s main orchestration point.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file contains the Markdown export logic used to generate per-page outputs (output filenames end with .md), which is directly relevant to JSONL/Markdown compatibility concerns the agent is checking.
- It demonstrates how existing conversion utilities are wired together:
  - Dynamic method loading (via available_methods and parse_method_arg) shows how JSONL/Markdown workflows could be extended or validated for compatibility with external tooling.
  - The remove_text flow uses render_pdf_to_base64png and convert_image_to_pdf_bytes to produce a text-free PDF, a path that may impact downstream JSONL formatting expectations if image-based or text-extracted data is used.
  - The concurrent processing and output organization logic informs how to structure or modify export formats and how to handle multi-page, multi-repeat outputs in a stable, observable way.
- Key touchpoints for modification:
  - process_pdf: where Markdown content is produced and written; a primary target for ensuring JSONL/Markdown compatibility or adding alternative export formats.
  - process_pdfs: where per-page/per-repeat expansion and output path structure are decided; useful for adjusting how data is serialized or how metadata is attached to outputs.
  - remove_text workflow: affects the content that downstream tooling would parse or index; relevant if JSONL embeddings or Markdown metadata need to reflect text-free vs. text-rich pages.
- Overall, this file is a practical centerpiece for understanding how PDFs are converted and exported to Markdown, with explicit hooks for extending formats or adjusting export semantics to fit a JSONL/Markdown compatibility plan.

output.pr_body:
Fixes #162

Changes proposed in this pull request:
- Add `jsonl_to_markdown.py` utility script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format
- Script extracts the 'text' field from each line in a JSONL file and saves it as a separate Markdown file
- Include proper error handling for JSON decoding and file operations
- Add example usage and documentation

Notes:
- This utility is designed to operate on any JSONL file where each line is a JSON object containing a text payload under the 'text' key. It will create one Markdown file per input JSONL line.
- It should gracefully skip or report problematic lines (e.g., invalid JSON) without aborting the entire run.
- Suggested tests should cover: normal usage with valid lines, lines missing 'text', invalid JSON lines, and non-existent input/output paths.

How to use (basic):
- python scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py --input data/sample.jsonl --output markdown_out --text-field text

This PR adds minimal surface area changes and should not affect existing PDF->Markdown export workflows. It adds an independent utility that can be used for preprocessing JSONL datasets for downstream documentation or testing workflows.
output.masca_optimized:
MASCA Analysis focused on this PR:

Scope and Intent
- Introduces a new standalone utility: scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py.
- Primary goal: convert a JSONL file to a set of Markdown files by extracting the 'text' field per line.
- No changes to core OCR/markdown export pipelines; this is a lightweight, auxiliary tool intended for data preparation and testing scenarios.

Impact Assessment
- Code footprint: small, self-contained script; low risk to existing modules.
- Dependencies: standard Python library only (likely json, pathlib, argparse), so compatibility is high across environments.
- Performance: Line-by-line processing; suitable for typical JSONL sizes used in tests. For very large files, streaming and batching considerations can be added later.

Potential Risks and Mitigations
- Missing text fields: script should handle gracefully (skip or log). Proposed behavior: skip lines without a usable text field but optionally allow forcing an empty markdown write if needed.
- Invalid JSON lines: robust error handling to log the issue and continue processing remaining lines.
- Output collisions: by default, generate deterministic filenames (e.g., 000001.md, 000002.md) to avoid collisions; provide an option to customize naming.
- Encoding issues: default to UTF-8 with an option to specify another encoding; handle decoding errors gracefully.

Testing Strategy (recommendations)
- Add tests under tests/ for:
  - Normal case: valid JSONL with text fields -> N outputs with correct content.
  - Missing text field: lines without 'text' are skipped or handled per option.
  - Invalid JSON: test that the script continues and reports errors without crashing.
  - Non-existent input file: proper error message and exit code.
  - Custom text-field name: verify that alternative field names work when specified.

Documentation and UX
- Include docstring in the new module detailing usage, arguments, and edge cases.
- Add a short README snippet or docs section describing the intended usage patterns and example commands.
- Consider integrating with existing docs that cover data preparation pipelines, to help users discover this utility.

files_to_modify.reason: Add new standalone utility to convert JSONL lines to Markdown by extracting the 'text' field per line; minimal risk and widely useful for data prep and testing.

functions_to_modify.reason: New module-level function implementing the core logic: read JSONL, extract text from each line, write per-line Markdown files with robust error handling.

output.analysis_summary:
Summary of analysis:
- Located existing JSONL sample (olmocr/bench/sample_data/dataset.jsonl) and recognized the 'text' field as the primary content carrier, which aligns with the intended behavior of the new script.
- Found no current jsonl_to_markdown utility, so this PR proposes a new, self-contained script at scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py.
- The new script should be independent of the existing PDF->Markdown conversion pipeline (olmocr/bench/convert.py) while complementing data prep workflows used in testing or dataset preparation.
- Key integration points include clear input/output semantics, robust error handling, and deterministic output naming for easy downstream usage.
- Proposed tests and documentation enhancements are recommended to ensure reliability and usability.
- Overall, this is a low-risk, high-value utility addition that should be straightforward to implement and maintain.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```

Includes robust error handling for invalid JSON and missing fields, and supports configurable text field name and output encoding.
